name: Pho Bo (Vietnamese Beef Pho)
description: Aromatic beef broth with rice noodles, thinly sliced beef, bean sprouts, basil, and lime.
instructions: Char onions and ginger over an open flame until blackened. Toast star anise, cinnamon, and cloves in a dry pan.
Blanch bones in boiling water 5 minutes, drain, and rinse.
Simmer bones with charred aromatics and toasted spices in 2L water for 1 hour, skimming froth.
Season broth with fish sauce and rock sugar. Strain.
Cook rice noodles according to package; drain.
Slice beef paper-thin. Place noodles in bowls, top with raw beef, ladle boiling broth over (it cooks the beef). Serve with bean sprouts, basil, lime, hoisin, and sriracha.
tips: Charring the onion and ginger gives pho its distinctive smoky sweetness.
The broth must be boiling when ladled to cook the raw beef.

name: Banh Mi
description: Vietnamese crusty baguette sandwich with pork, pickled daikon and carrot, jalapeño, and cilantro.
instructions: Quick-pickle daikon and carrot: julienne, toss with vinegar, sugar, and salt, rest 20 minutes.
Season pork with soy sauce and fish sauce. Pan-fry or grill until cooked, slice thinly.
Split baguettes lengthwise, toast until crispy outside but soft inside.
Spread pate on one side and mayo on the other.
Layer sliced pork, pickled vegetables, cucumber slices, jalapeño, and cilantro.
Press lightly and serve.
tips: The bread should be crispy and light — a proper Vietnamese baguette, not a dense French one.
The contrast of rich meat and tangy pickles is what makes banh mi special.

name: Bun Cha
description: Hanoi-style grilled pork patties and belly with rice noodles, herbs, and a tangy dipping broth.
instructions: Mix ground pork with 1 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, and minced garlic. Form into small patties.
Slice pork belly thin, marinate with 1 tbsp fish sauce and sugar.
Grill patties and belly slices over high heat until charred.
Make dipping broth: mix remaining fish sauce, sugar, vinegar, lime juice, sliced chili, and 100ml warm water. Add julienned carrot.
Cook rice vermicelli, drain and rinse cold.
Serve noodles and herbs on a plate, grilled pork on the side, and dipping broth in a bowl.
tips: The charring on the pork is essential — it should be slightly caramelized, not just cooked.
Dip the noodles and pork into the broth as you eat.

name: Goi Cuon (Fresh Spring Rolls)
description: Vietnamese fresh rice paper rolls with shrimp, pork, vermicelli, herbs, and peanut dipping sauce.
instructions: Cook vermicelli, drain and cool. Slice pork thinly. Halve shrimp lengthwise.
Make peanut sauce: warm hoisin with peanut butter, garlic, chili, and enough water to thin. Add lime juice.
Dip one rice paper in warm water until just pliable (5 seconds, no more).
Place lettuce, herbs, vermicelli, and pork in the lower third. Fold bottom up, fold sides in.
Place shrimp along the fold line (pink side against the wrapper so they show through).
Roll tightly. Serve with peanut dipping sauce.
tips: Do not over-soak the rice paper — it becomes too soft and tears. Just 5 seconds.
Place shrimp facing out so the pink shows through the translucent wrapper.

name: Cao Lau
description: Hoi An specialty of thick chewy noodles with pork, greens, crispy croutons, and a light broth.

ingredients.name: cao lau noodles or thick udon

instructions: Marinate pork in soy sauce, five-spice, sugar, and garlic for 30 minutes. Roast at 200C for 20 minutes. Slice thinly.
Fry wonton wrapper strips in oil until crispy for croutons.
Cook noodles according to package; drain.
Mix a small amount of soy sauce with a splash of the pork cooking juices for a light sauce.
Assemble bowls: noodles, a few spoonfuls of sauce (not soupy), sliced pork, bean sprouts, and lettuce.
Top with crispy croutons and fresh herbs. Squeeze lime over.
tips: Cao lau is served with very little liquid — it is not a soup.
The crispy croutons add essential texture contrast.

name: Com Tam (Broken Rice)
description: Vietnamese broken rice with grilled pork chop, fried egg, pickled vegetables, and fish sauce dressing.
instructions: Blend lemongrass, garlic, shallot, 2 tbsp fish sauce, 1 tbsp sugar, and oil. Marinate pork chops 1 hour.
Cook broken rice (cook like regular rice with slightly less water).
Grill pork chops over high heat until charred and cooked through, about 5 minutes per side.
Fry eggs sunny-side-up.
Make nuoc cham: mix remaining fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, chili, and 3 tbsp water.
Plate: rice, grilled pork, fried egg, cucumber, tomato, pickled vegetables. Drizzle nuoc cham.
tips: Broken rice has a unique texture — regular rice is not a substitute.
The nuoc cham dressing ties everything together — it should be sweet, salty, sour, and spicy.

name: Bun Bo Hue
description: Spicy Vietnamese beef noodle soup from Hue with lemongrass, shrimp paste, and thick rice noodles.
instructions: Blanch beef and pork bones, drain and rinse. Cover with fresh water and simmer 1.5 hours.
Pound lemongrass, add to broth along with charred onion. Simmer.
Mix shrimp paste with chili oil, add to broth. Season with fish sauce and sugar.
Remove beef shank, slice thinly. Strain broth.
Cook noodles according to package; drain into bowls.
Ladle broth over noodles, top with beef slices. Serve with bean sprouts, basil, banana blossom, lime, and chili.
tips: Bun bo Hue should be noticeably spicy — the chili oil is not optional.
Shrimp paste gives the broth its unique funky depth.

name: Canh Chua (Vietnamese Sour Soup)
description: Tangy tamarind soup with fish, tomatoes, pineapple, okra, and bean sprouts.
instructions: Dissolve tamarind paste in 1L of warm water, strain out solids.
Bring tamarind broth to a simmer, add tomato wedges and pineapple chunks.
Add sliced taro stem or celery, and okra. Simmer 5 minutes.
Add fish pieces (cut into chunks), cook 3-4 minutes until just done.
Season with fish sauce and sugar. Add bean sprouts.
Garnish with fried garlic, Thai basil, and sliced chili. Serve with steamed rice.
tips: Add fish last — it breaks apart if cooked too long.
The soup should be notably sour — adjust tamarind to taste.

name: Banh Xeo
description: Crispy Vietnamese crepe filled with shrimp, pork, bean sprouts, and herbs, wrapped in lettuce.
instructions: Mix rice flour, coconut milk, turmeric, a pinch of salt, and enough water for a thin batter. Rest 15 minutes.
Make dipping sauce (nuoc cham): fish sauce, sugar, lime juice, water, and sliced chili.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan. Add pork and shrimp, cook 2 minutes.
Pour a thin layer of batter, swirl to coat. Add bean sprouts and spring onion.
Cover and cook 3-4 minutes until bottom is crispy and golden.
Fold in half. Wrap pieces of crepe with lettuce and herbs, dip in nuoc cham.
tips: The crepe should be paper-thin and shatteringly crispy.
Wrapping in lettuce and herbs before dipping is the traditional way to eat it.

name: Vietnamese Lemongrass Chicken
description: Caramelized chicken stir-fried with lemongrass, garlic, and chili in a sweet fish sauce glaze.
instructions: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces. Mince lemongrass, garlic, shallot, and chili.
Marinate chicken with fish sauce, sugar, lemongrass, garlic, and pepper for 30 minutes.
Heat oil in a wok until very hot. Add chicken in a single layer, cook without stirring 3 minutes for caramelization.
Stir-fry 3-4 minutes more until chicken is cooked and sauce is sticky.
Add sliced chili and shallot, toss 1 minute.
Garnish with spring onion, serve with steamed rice.
tips: Do not stir immediately — let the chicken caramelize for a few minutes first.
Use the tender inner part of the lemongrass stalk only.
